speaker: interviewer
text: You hear me? This is roll eight. This is roll eight with Hellmuth Szprycer. Roll eight with Hellmuth Szprycer. Outside interview. Outside interview in Birkenau, on January 28. On location, on-site interview.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we were standing here. It was a lot of snow, ice. And you know, we had to be counted.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when we were counted, it was in the moment, we had our caps on. But the blockalteste, they counted us first. You know? One, two, three, four. How many here? We were maybe 200, or so. And then when he counted, he had a bit of paper. He's counting the tape. He's walking.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then the SS men came up, the hauptscharfuhrer. And then when the hauptscharfuhrer came up, we had to say-- [SPEAKING GERMAN]

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had to take them up. And you go together like this. And then the SS men start counting us. You know, the same as the blockalteste. And if there were one or two people next to us, old people, falling down dying, you know? Because they took a half an hour, sometimes an hour. Many, many would die.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- [SPEAKING GERMAN] --they couldn't do that. Like in the army, at the beginning, when they get to some of them, they couldn't follow. Especially the old people couldn't follow. What happened when all the counting was done from all the camp, you can imagine all these barracks. It took an hour, an hour and a half. Then the SS went all around and had the whole counting from the camp, went to Obersturmfuhrer Schwarzhuber, who was on the front, the commandant.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they gave the full counting of the camp to him. But it was all made in a manner of discipline. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: But sometimes it happened, when we were standing, that suddenly there was alarm. Somebody was running away. I remember here, staying here sometimes five, six hours in winter, until they found those people, the ones who escaped.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were, can you imagine, six hours? You can imagine how cold it is now. Standing here, and I went through that as a child, and I'm still alive? it's unbelievable.

words.text: You can imagine how cold it is now.
I went through that as a

speaker: interviewee
text: Because many people fall dead. And after six hours, when they found them, they brought them into the camp. And they were set on the front of the camp. And they were sat on chairs. They went out with SS dogs to fetch them. And they were already dead. They shot them through their mouths, eyes. They brought them in and put them on there, three of them, and tied them up on the chair. And then they say-- [SPEAKING GERMAN]. Then we were allowed to retire from our appel. But we had to go, all, up there, to have a look what happened to these prisoners. And they said, [GERMAN], if you do the same what these three did, you will also end up with the same thing. This was every day, there were those surprises. You got out of the barracks. Each morning, there were about 20 bodies lying here, in front of the barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: These 20 bodies, can you imagine? In front of every. They were yellow. They were green, dead.

speaker: interviewee
text: But you know, what surprised me a lot, which I'm thinking about now, when you looked at these poor people, you know, because nobody closed their eyes when they died. You know, their eyes were open. And they looked happy. They laughed, like they were laughing. They were [NON-ENGLISH]. They were happy they died. Because for them, it was marvelous. You saw it in their faces that they were happy. They were not people who, like, usually people when they die, you see. Their eyes were really laughing. Like they finished and I'm happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because they waited for this. Everybody here, they was working next to us. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: The half of us, we were dead. So you didn't want to accept it. Because that's why you fought through it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So every day, in the morning, that was the same thing, were people dead in front of the barracks. And then they had to lie there because when they had the appel to count them, then the SS allowed them to put them in the back. When they were put in the back, right there in the back, there was a big, like you could put coal on it, were all corpse. Then the [NON-ENGLISH] with the wheels fetched them. And they put them on the what's it called?

speaker: interviewee
text: Full of the whole lot. And they took them through there, through the gate. And up to the gas chambers, to be burned. Because as you can see, the gas chambers were around here. Now you can really, really notice how near-- because people say that you see them burning. Look, we're in the middle of it. In the middle of it, it's smelling every day. The flames, day and night. The smell, we got used to. Because it was a funny smell. When you burn human meat-- let's say, human beings-- it's a funny smell. You keep that smell. But after you lived here for a year, you got used to it. You got used to it. But you did hear, at nighttime, when you did some of the gassing at night, you hear the people crying and shouting. You hear the sound. Look, you're standing here yourself now. You're witnessing this, witnessing how quiet this is here. There is a wind. But if somebody would be over there and shout, you hear them over here. You hear them. This is like, an echo. I don't know. Every day you have that sort of-- what's it called-- surprise. And you didn't know what's going to happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell a little more about in relation to your job, living in this barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. You see, actually, then I got my job. At first, when I came here, I looked towards the orchestra. That must have been in the middle there, the barracks there, where there was the orchestra playing. And I went directly there to ask them if they were the half from the Ghetto Swingers from the Theresienstadt. And I met two of them. I went in there. Oh, [NON-ENGLISH] and we were so happy. And I start playing with them and singing with them. And there was also, the woman, Elsa, who lives in Israel. She wrote a book, where they took the baby away from her. Elsa, I don't know the second name. When I went to Israel for the Holocaust Survivor once, I met her there. And she remembers me as a [NON-ENGLISH]. And that woman, something awful happened to her. You know, she had a baby born in this camp here. And Mengele wanted to take away the baby from her.

words.text: must have been in the middle

speaker: interviewer
text: It's a known story. And I knew it. I saw it. And when she went to appel, she was hiding it under her-- what's it called?

speaker: interviewee
text: And Mengele found it. And the story is on the end, he was treating her so badly, that he tried something with her, he experimented. He tied up her two bosoms with strings. [SOBBING] With strings, that she couldn't give milk to her child. She explained it, to see how long that child could exist without her milk. I think it was seven days. She tried, always, to feed it. And it was impossible. They made some milk out of something. And they put it into the-- and they feed it that little kid. But that little baby couldn't survive. And in the end, her friends said, you know, your baby's dying. And she didn't want to accept it. And she said, listen, let me take away that kid, and give it an injection or something, to kill it because there was somebody from the medic hospital, which we had. And Elsa didn't want to give it. And she said, if we don't give it, you will go also, to the gas chamber with that kid. And she had to give her baby. She's known. She explains it. She is a known person in Israel. And that's why she was alive. I met her. You see, such things Mengele did. And this is unbelievable. This happened here in this camp. And I know. Mengele came with his [NON-ENGLISH]. And that was in the 14th of July, '44. And he didn't come in for his experiment. He came in, and he was so set on making a selection. And it was in front of the barracks. They were a bit changed, the barracks. I don't know how they did it the day before. There was a table here. And he was sitting on the table. And we had to undress, put our clothes in here. And we had to come out in front of him. And he was usually said to-- when you came in front of him, naked-- he looked at you and he said, left. And he said, right. And we had to go back and dress again.

words.text: right. And we had to go

speaker: interviewer
text: So by the time that selection was finished, we knew that was the day. The ones who were, I knew. I came out. I was standing with my friends here. And we were talking to each other. And we said, what's happening? What's happening? Oh yeah, [NON-ENGLISH]. Now it's left, gas chamber.

speaker: interviewee
text: And most of the boys didn't even say anything. They were with their families. Because there were, what, 350 children here. And the families who were the women, he did the selection in different places in the women barracks. So everybody was moving about like mad. So at that moment, when I came out, suddenly, I don't know how I did it. I saw Mengele standing there, in the middle with Schwarzhuber. You know, six feet tall, both of them. I said, I've got to go up to them. And my friend said to me, a boy standing also there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you mad? He's going to shoot you. I said, no. So I went up to Mengele. He was stood there. And I said--

speaker: interviewee
text: [SPEAKING GERMAN] Let me be alive. I want to work for you. I want to clean your shoes. I can do a lot of work. I'm strong. I begged for my life.

words.text: I want to work for you.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he looked at me. You  know, like this I was, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SPEAKING GERMAN] I had already my [NON-ENGLISH] uniform from the gate. And he looked at me and he said, [SPEAKING GERMAN]

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, I'm from Berlin. Ach, ein Berliner? I said, ja, ein Berliner. Like this, you know? Tall men looked down on me. OK--

speaker: interviewee
text: [SPEAKING GERMAN] I turn around. First of all, I said danke, thank you. And I turned around, and I marched away. As I marched away, I got near to my friend, who was standing aside there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Harry, something, I don't remember. He was crying. He said, Hellmuth, can't you do the same for me? Mengele was still standing quite far away.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was standing in the middle. I said, oh I will try, I said. So I had the [NON-ENGLISH]. I don't know. I went up again, to him. I said, [SPEAKING GERMAN]

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you want now? I said, [SPEAKING GERMAN].

speaker: interviewee
text: Berliner, Berliner, [SPEAKING GERMAN], I will do something. He said, I'll take two matches. And I will put them together. And the one who pulls the longest match will go as a [NON-ENGLISH] to the D Lager. And the other one goes into the gas chamber. I said [SPEAKING GERMAN].

speaker: interviewer
text: Anyhow, what happens? I pulled the long match. At that moment, I felt happy.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because you know, life, you think life, you think you're not in the gas chamber. He was frightened. I turned around again. And I went back. And that boy was crying so, like hell. I couldn't. I couldn't. The third time back. [SPEAKING GERMAN]

speaker: interviewer
text: He said, what do you want again now?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SPEAKING GERMAN] Berliner, Berliner. He was really annoyed. But the same way, I don't know, there was a look in his face.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, a strong look. Schwarzhuber, maybe like, they said, what's happening here? And he said to me, I will give him also work in the kitchen. I said,

speaker: interviewee
text: [SPEAKING GERMAN], Danke. I turned around. I went to tell it to him, he was so happy. I met him two years later in London. But the same time as we were safe, there were 90 boys from the 350, also came to the selection, who were also for the gas chamber, were also spared. And that is, until now, nobody knows why. A possibility, 55 years later we got together in Czechoslovakia with the group. They said, because you talked to Mengele and this, that he changed his mind and saved, probably, the other 90 as well. That is the selection from 1944, in July the 14 that these boys of 90 were saved out of the selection. I marched a few days before into the D Lager. And they followed then, a few days after, into the D Lager as well. As you can see, this is D Lager. Here was my work the last six months for my stay in Birkenau. I had to come every morning here and report to Rabetski, Hauptscharfuhrer Rabetski, 178th 35, [NON-ENGLISH]. That means reports on the gate. This was my work here day by day, in Birkenau. In the morning, at 6 o'clock, Obersturmfuhrer Schwarzhuber came then as well, with his motorcycle. And left the motorcycle here. I had to also report to him, Obersturmfuhrer, and, Berliner, clean the motorcycle. Take the motorcycle to clean it. This was the barrack from him, it was a big barrack in wood for the officers. The kommandos, at 6 o'clock, had to march out here. As you can see, on the left there, there was the orchestra playing the march music. And they marched out here. There was two other [NON-ENGLISH] with me here on the gate. We had to stay here. But I show you left, and the other one on the right, or two on this side. And we had to say links, rechts, links, rechts, links, rechts. Left, right, left, right. The commandos walked out wherever they had to go, to build the new ramp, and so on, whatever. The way they were looking, the 67, 50 kilos they weighed. And they could move about unbelievable. But then they went out, but they were also sometimes the Alsactian dogs standing here. Usually it was six, seven. And they went along with the commandos. And the SS also went with them, that nobody could escape. And my daily work was here on the gate, to clean the shoes from the commandant, to clean his clothes when he came in. Sometimes I  had to take messages to the commandant too. And my daily work was here as a [NON-ENGLISH] when he arrived, some other SS arrived. There was, sometimes, days here, you had four or five big SS officers standing here, coming here, meeting here, Kramer. Kramer, at that at that moment, was the commandant from Birkenau, Kramer. And Hoss came. They all came, sometimes, here. And they met together to laugh and enjoy themselves. And I had to clean all their motorcycles. As you see, the camp, how cold it is now, and how cold it was then. Sometimes when Schwarzhuber came into his office, in the back, there was a small kitchen. The [NON-ENGLISH], there was [NON-ENGLISH] SS who he had to cook some food for him. Eggs and sometimes with some pork. And you know, with the french fried. And he gave some to me as well. So this is the life on the D Lager. As you can see, I'm standing here. I can't believe myself after 52 years, that I'm back here. And then telling the story to the American television. It's unbelievable. It's not thinkable, what happened here in those days. As you see, in those days, I was 13 and 1/2 years of age, a little kid. A healthy kid in one way, who went through life fighting for it. And my work here was cleaning shoes, cleaning motorcycles, messages. And I survived. It is today, unbelievable. It's a miracle that I'm here. I stayed on this gate. I sat down there because we sometimes had a half an hour off in the sunshine, when it was not winter, and at the grass which was in there. And sat in the sun and enjoyed my life, as those days, life was precious every day. You survived. And you knew that the gate closed and the SS went away. And this was the highest. Then you knew one more day. In those days, death walked aside of you. So life was precious. Yes, at '44 August, I came here with the orchestra, Rabetski. And we marched in from the D Lager. Through the back, through the back there. Not this. And we arrived there. And they must have put up stands where the SS could sit. And that was in the woods. And all of the big men were here. Himmler, the rest, Kramer, Schwarzhuber, Mengele, and they had to play to them, music. At that moment, I also whistled a march song. But when it was finished, I did my whistling. And they played on. Then they had a meeting. The meeting was that Hitler probably ordered that the gassing for the Jews from Hungary which are coming in doesn't go fast enough. They had to do it quicker. There was a big argument. And they got together to talk about it. I came from there. When they played, again, and they were occupied, I walked around a bit like I'm doing now. As a little child, from 13 and 1/2, 14, I was [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: That means to say, I wanted to see what's happening. What is this here? And as I came this way over the porch, or this way, it doesn't matter, I mean.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I saw a man standing on the entrance. But you've got to remember, where you're standing now, with your camera, this was a building, quite a nice building. It looked like a villa.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I came in. And he stood there. I mean, now I know what danger I was in. And I said to him-- I don't know if the entrance was here or the entrance was there. I went in. And he said to me, [SPEAKING YIDDISH]. And I said yes. He spoke Yiddish. So I said, [NON-ENGLISH]? So he said, they're in the gas chamber. I said, what do you mean, in the gas chamber? That's where they are killing the people, gassing them and burning them. So I said, what's happening? So he told me-- I can remember-- but just I had a matter of things. This was the-- what's it called-- the room. They came in there.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was the room with a door, where the doors were. This was the other part of it, as you can see. There, this where they came in. And that's probably the undressing hall. They undressed there. And they had even to hang up their coats. I didn't see all that. But this is later on. It was mentioned like this. But all I saw here was that room. And that is the gas chamber. They came in from there naked. And when I ask him, I said, there is still some blood there. He said, yes, because we have to clean it quick. By the time the next commando comes in, the next people from Hungary, wherever, they have to clean it up so they don't see the blood.

speaker: interviewer
text: Otherwise they sense something. You see, the SS, the systems, that they all build it up, the SS, knew that had to go very clean. You know? That nothing could have been stopped. So cleaned it up, that it looked like the real shower hall. You know? So what happened is I saw and I said to him, I said, what was it then, these metal doors? So he said, yes, metal doors. They went in at the other side.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this metal door was closed. And they filled them up as much as possible. And when they were in there, he said, the gas was running, Zyklon B, at that time. And the deaths, the fight against the deaths must've taken 50 minutes, he told me. There was shouting going on, crying going on. He heard it all. He spoke some Yiddish to me. Nothing went on at that moment. You see, the sonderkommando, it's like being in a theater. They didn't come in to the front. They came in in the back. Because in the back, they had to do their job. In the front, our people came in. Here this just was the end stage.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he said, when they opened the door, when they opened the door, some people were falling out. But the rest, inside, was full of people, right up to the top to the-- what's it called? To the ceiling. And it was all heavy people, heavy people who were on top.

speaker: interviewee
text: And all the little ones and the weak ones were on the bottom. And he said, a lot of ones had big stomachs, probably because of the gas in them. And he said, the SS took the carbines, he said, and pushed it in, that the air should go out.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that it was all blood which also was spitting out. He said, people, he saw them hanging, some of them, on the wall. Scratching, because these walls had a lot of scratch marks from fingernails and so on. He said by the time when it was opened, and they got them out-- I said, you took them out with your hands? He said, no. So they had, on the side, he has shown me, on the side, they were against the wall. There were a lot of metal things. I said, what is this all? So I saw long metal things, with scissors. He said, we had to take that round their neck and pull them out. Ah, I said, that's the way. And he said, and when they were pulled out, before we put them into the burning-- that is probably here, to burn them-- the fire, or the ovens-- we had to take the-- what's it called-- there was a little table there where they took the teeth out. You know? And from women, they also searched them. And they told me all that. You know? And sometimes there were hidden things there as well in their underneath. And everything was before they went into the-- what's it called-- to be burned, everything was searched. And I said to myself, how is it possible they were dead already? And then you still touched those people? They didn't give them the dignity to die proper and take with them their own tooth. They still touched the bodies and didn't leave it alone? Took their belongings, their private belongings from them, from their teeth and all that? How is it possible that a thing like this has happened? How is it possible? I said to him, I didn't understand.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 13 and 1/2. I said, This is the [SPEAKING YIDDISH]. He showed me then, the ovens. They put them through the back there, and they pushed them in. And then the flames came up, he said. And it was gone.

speaker: interviewer
text: But how, today, I'm here. And I say to myself, 52 years later, how is that possible? How could the Germans have done a thing like this? The German people, who were so intelligent and such a race, from high culture, Mozart, Schubert, whatever, gassed our people like that? And took them away from this world like this? How is it possible? How can people behave that way? Why? I'm asking the question now, why has that happened? Why? What was behind it? Why? This is the most monstrous thing whichever happened in this world.

speaker: interviewee
text: Monstrous. And I want to say, because I'm standing here now, after 52 years, to everybody, to everybody, beg them that it should never happen again. It should never happen again for their own children as well. Beg these people to look and see, now, what you see here now, the world to tell them that it should never. I beg them to listen to me. Please listen to me and don't ever let it happen again. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: Oy vey, where are you all? My oma, mein opa, mein sisters, my family. My whole people, where are you? Where are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They are here. They are looking at us. I'm here to tell it because they can't say anything. And I'm saying it open. Listen. Listen to them. Oy, yai, yai.
